text: Good morning! Feel free to browse. That leather bag is handmade, top of the range. Sixty euros.
It is lovely, but sixty is a little pricey. The stall round the corner had a similar one for far less.
Ah, but those are knock-offs. Mine is real leather, considerably better made. It will last for years.
I can see the quality. But I am on a tight budget. Could you do forty?
Forty is too low, my friend. But because you have a good eye, I will say fifty. That is real value for money.
Let us meet in the middle. Forty-five, and you have a deal.
You drive a hard bargain! Forty-five it is. At that price, it is almost a steal.
Wonderful. The more we talked, the better the price got. Thank you!

Welcome to the old market, my friend. Let me give you one honest tip before you start. Up here, by the main gate, everything is considerably more expensive, and a lot of it is overpriced. Those 'handmade' bowls? Most are knock-offs, far cheaper just two streets down. The marked prices are only a starting point, so do not be shy to haggle. A friendly shopper who takes their time can usually talk me down by a third, sometimes more. And remember: the cheapest stall is not always the best. A slightly pricier piece that is properly made is often far better value for money than a bargain that breaks on the plane home.

I used to be a terrible shopper when I travelled. I would pay the first price out of politeness, and I came home with a suitcase full of overpriced rubbish. These days I am far more careful. I browse slowly, I never look too keen, and I am always ready to haggle. The more relaxed I am, the more the price seems to drop. But the biggest change is how I think about money now. I no longer chase the cheapest thing, because the cheapest is often a false economy. I look for value for money, for the piece that is considerably better made and will still mean something to me years later.
